coredata.dc:title: Intelligently design primary aromatic amines derived carbon dots for optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging detection of nitrite based on specific diazo coupling
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Yumin
Yang, Zhenhua
Dong, Wenjuan
Liu, Yang
Song, Shengmei
Hu, Qin
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
Gong, Xiaojuan
coredata.dc:description: Primary aromatic amines derived carbon dots (PAA-CDs) with the protonated amino groups and high quantum yield of 46% were favorably obtained by one-step solvothermal treatment of m-phenylenediamine (m-PDA) in acidic environment. The interaction between the PAA-CDs and nitrite (NO2 -) was inherited the characteristic reaction of m-PDA (a primary aromatic amine) and NO2 -, resulting in strong fluorescence quenching and obvious absorption variation of the PAA-CDs. Meanwhile, a chromogenic reaction of diazo coupling can cause significant color changes. Hence, the PAA-CDs were developed for an optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging sensor for NO2 - detection in the range of 3.0 ~ 40.0 μM with high selectivity, good sensitivity, and excellent anti-interference capability. A limit of detection (LOD) of 0.024 μM and 0.16 μM was implemented by fluorometry and colorimetry, respectively. For smartphone imaging colorimetry, the LODs of 0.46 μM (visible color) and 0.99 μM (fluorescence color) were acquired. More importantly, the established sensor has been successfully applied for the dynamic detection of NO2 - in various food samples with the satisfying results. A smartphone imaging colorimetry method based on the CDs was firstly proposed to visually and quantitatively detect NO2 -, which will broaden the application range of the CDs in food safety inspection.

Surface structures of the PAA-CDs. (a) FT-IR spectra of m-PDA and the PAA-CDs. (b) XPS survey scan, (c) C 1s XPS spectrum, and (d) N 1s XPS spectrum of the PAA-CDs.

Optical properties of the PAA-CDs. (a) UV–vis absorption (purple section), fluorescence excitation (cyan section), and emission (green section) spectra of the PAA-CDs (0.01 mg mL−1). The inserted images display the photos of the PAA-CDs under daylight (left) and UV lamp (right). (b) Fluorescence emission behavior of the PAA-CDs at different excitation wavelengths.

Optimization and comparison for NO2 - sensing system based on the PAA-CDs. Optimization of (a) HCl concentration and (b) reaction time of the PAA-CDs in HCl solution before and after the addition of NO2 -. Comparison of (c) relative intensity (PL intensity and absorbance) and (d) relative value (the visible image's ratio of H/L and the fluorescence image's H) of the PAA-CDs (0.01 mg mL−1) in the presence of the metal ions (1.0 mM) or other anions (1.0 mM) before and after the addition of NO2 - (40 μM) in HCl solution (0.01 M).

The PAA-CDs for optical dual-mode detection of NO2 -. (a) Fluorescence and (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of the PAA-CDs (0.01 mg mL−1) after adding different concentrations of NO2 - in HCl (0.01 M). Plot of (b) fluorescence intensity and (d) absorbance of the PAA-CDs versus NO2 - concentrations.

The PAA-CDs for the smartphone imaging detection of NO2 -. The images and color information (HSL) of the PAA-CDs after adding different concentrations of NO2 - under (a) visible light and (b) ultraviolet light. Calibration curves obtained by plotting (c) H/L or (d) H/H0 against NO2 - concentration.

Interaction between the PAA-CDs and NO2 - in HCl (0.01 M). (a) Absorption spectra of the PAA-CDs in the absence and presence of NO2 -. (b) Absorbance of m-PDA in HCl (0.01 M) with different concentrations of NO2 - (0, 5, 10, 30, and 50 μM). The inserted images show the corresponding color variations. (c) FT-IR spectra of the PAA-CDs, PAA-CDs in HCl, and the mixture of the PAA-CDs and NO2 - in HCl. (d) N 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of the PAA-CDs without and with NO2 -.

Schematic illustration of (a) the diazo coupling between m-PDA and NO2 - and (b) the synthetic process of the PAA-CDs.

Schematic diagram of the PAA-CDs for the detection of NO2 - via the smartphone imaging colorimetry. Step 1: take photos by smartphone camera; Step 2: identify colors and output HSL values by the smartphone software (Color Identification); Step 3: enter values and make charts by the smartphone software (WPS Office).

head.ce:title: Intelligently design primary aromatic amines derived carbon dots for optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging detection of nitrite based on specific diazo coupling

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Primary aromatic amines derived carbon dots (PAA-CDs) with the protonated amino groups and high quantum yield of 46% were favorably obtained by one-step solvothermal treatment of m-phenylenediamine (m-PDA) in acidic environment. The interaction between the PAA-CDs and nitrite (NO2 -) was inherited the characteristic reaction of m-PDA (a primary aromatic amine) and NO2 -, resulting in strong fluorescence quenching and obvious absorption variation of the PAA-CDs. Meanwhile, a chromogenic reaction of diazo coupling can cause significant color changes. Hence, the PAA-CDs were developed for an optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging sensor for NO2 - detection in the range of 3.0 ~ 40.0 μM with high selectivity, good sensitivity, and excellent anti-interference capability. A limit of detection (LOD) of 0.024 μM and 0.16 μM was implemented by fluorometry and colorimetry, respectively. For smartphone imaging colorimetry, the LODs of 0.46 μM (visible color) and 0.99 μM (fluorescence color) were acquired. More importantly, the established sensor has been successfully applied for the dynamic detection of NO2 - in various food samples with the satisfying results. A smartphone imaging colorimetry method based on the CDs was firstly proposed to visually and quantitatively detect NO2 -, which will broaden the application range of the CDs in food safety inspection.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Primary aromatic amines derived carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite (NO2 -), a kind of food additive, is widely used in foods to retain freshness (antioxidants) or inhibit microbial growth (preservative agents) (Gassara et al., 2016). However, the additive amount of NO2 - is strictly limited, attributing to that the large ingestion of NO2 - can cause serious damage to human health. NO2 - can react with amides or secondary amines of the stomach to form carcinogenic N-nitrosamines and combine with hemoglobin to generate methemoglobin that can induce the body hypoxia (Wolff and Wasserman, 1972; Avery, 1999). To better guarantee the quality and safety of food, it is necessary to accurately measure NO2 - in food. Numerous efforts have been undertaken for realizing the detection of NO2 -, including chromatography (Antczak-Chrobot et al., 2018), electrochemistry (Haldorai et al., 2016), and spectroscopy methods (Yu et al., 2021; Hou et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018). Of all these methods, the fluorometry and colorimetry methods are usually used to detect objects with the optical dual-signal output to increase the reliability of data (Liu et al., 2020). In particular, the fluorescence method is more widely used to detect NO2 - because of its low cost, high accuracy, and simple analysis (Wang et al., 2017).

Many materials used for the fluorimetric or colorimetric detection of NO2 - mainly participate in diazotization or nitrification reactions (Wang et al., 2017). Among a variety of fluorescence materials, carbon dots (CDs) have been considerably concerned due to their simple preparation, good water solubility, high photobleaching resistance, and low toxicity (Yao et al., 2019; Xia et al., 2019; Qu et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2020). Deng et al. (2021) prepared the red-fluorescence CDs by a one-step hydrothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine to realize NO2 - detection. Xiang et al. (Xiang et al., 2018) adopted a dual-emission sensor for the detection of NO2 - by coating CDs onto the dye-doped silica nanoparticles. Using two kinds of CDs, a dual-emissive fluorescence sensor was constructed to detect NO2 - (Liu et al., 2019). Although these CDs-based sensors for NO2 - detection have their own advantages, such as simple synthesis, long emission wavelength, or dual emission wavelength, these sensors can only obtain the detection results by the single-signal fluorescence output, which is difficult to avoid the occurrence of false positive results. This effect can be minimized by constructing a dual-signal or multi-signal output sensing system to detect NO2 - (Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022). Without combining with other materials, NO2 - can be detected by fluorimetric and colorimetric dual-mode only with the CDs containing amino groups. Jia et al. (2020) have fabricated the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) by the one-step hydrothermal method for the detection of NO2 - based on the decreased fluorescence intensity and absorbance, attributing to the interaction between NO2 - and the amino groups of the N-CDs. Generally, the fluorimetric or colorimetric sensing systems for NO2 - detection have obvious color changes under ultraviolet light or visible light, respectively. Although the visual sensor based on the CDs can be constructed to detect NO2 - via observing color changes (Zhan et al., 2019; Lo et al., 2017; Nam et al., 2018), its accuracy and sensitivity are unsatisfactory because it can only rely on naked eye to distinguish. Using a smartphone instead of a naked eye to differentiate the slight color change can not only realize on-site and real-time detection of objects, but also improve the accuracy of measurement data (Pol et al., 2019; Chu et al., 2020; Kong et al., 2019). Further, the data from the smartphone imaging under both visible and ultraviolet lights are more reliable than that from a single light source, which is similar with the optical dual-mode output sensor. Consequently, developing an optical dual-mode sensor for smartphone imaging detection of NO2 - has great theoretical significance and practical value.

Herein, primary aromatic amines derived carbon dots (PAA-CDs) were cleverly obtained by one-step solvothermal treatment of the single m-phenylenediamine (m-PDA) ( Scheme 1). Then, an optical sensor with dual-signal output (fluorescence intensity and absorbance) based on the PAA-CDs was established for the sensitive and precise detection of NO2 - (Scheme 1). Simultaneously, a smartphone imaging colorimetry with dual-mode (color changes under visible and ultraviolet lights) was implemented in the visual and quantitative detection of NO2 - (Scheme 1). Compared with most CDs-based sensors for NO2 - detection, the proposed method exhibits some significant superiorities: (1) the construction of the dual-mode sensor system based on the PAA-CDs is simple without the assistance of other materials; (2) the introduction of multi-signal output and intelligent identification improves the reliability of NO2 - detection results; (3) compared with some semi-quantitative detection of NO2 - achieved by the naked eye recognition, the combination of smartphone and sensor system is easier to achieve the quantitative detection of NO2 -. This work is expected to provide a novel idea for the design of dual-mode output and smartphone sensor system, promoting the rapid development of smartphone sensor field.

m-Phenylenediamine (m-PDA), hydrochloric acid (HCl), ethanol, sodium tetraborate, potassium ferrocyanide, zinc acetate, p-aminobenzene sulfonic acid, N-(1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine dihydrochloride, sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), glutathione (GSH), ascorbic acid (AA), ursolic acid (UA), oxalic acid (OA), citric acid (CA), all amino acids, and various inorganic metal salts were obtained from Aladdin Ltd (Shanghai, China). All reagents are analytical grade or above, which can be used without pretreatment. Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ cm) was applied throughout the experiments.

Morphologies of the PAA-CDs were observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM), atomic force microscope (AFM), and X-ray diffraction (XRD). Surface structures of the PAA-CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The thermal stability of the PAA-CDs was performed by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). Optical features of the PAA-CDs were measured by UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra. In addition, fluorescence lifetime, zeta potential, and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were implemented in detail. The correlative instrument information and specific parameters were referred to our previously published papers (Gong et al., 2015, 2016). All the images were captured on a smartphone (HUAWEI Nova 8) under visible and ultraviolet lights.

The PAA-CDs were synthesized by the solvothermal method. Briefly, 0.1 g of m-PDA was dissolved in 10 mL of mixed solvent (V(ethanol): V(ultrapure water) = 1:1) containing 0.54% HCl (36–37%, 150 μL). After 5 min of sonication, the clear precursor solution was sealed in a 50 mL Teflon autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The reaction solution was naturally cooled to room temperature in air and then purified by the dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) = 100–500 Da) against ultrapure water for 9 h with recharging ultrapure water every 3 h. Finally, the PAA-CDs solid powder was obtained by freeze-drying, which was redispersed in ultrapure water to prepare a certain concentration for further use.

The PL and UV–vis dual-mode detections were performed in hydrochloric acid solution (HCl, 10 mM) supplemented with 0.01 mg mL−1 PAA-CDs. Firstly, 2 μL of the PAA-CDs aqueous solution (10 mg mL−1) was fully mixed with 2.0 mL of HCl solution (0.01 M) in a test tube to prepare the PAA-CDs solution (0.01 mg mL−1). Then, different volumes of NO2 - (0.01 M) were separately introduced into the PAA-CDs solution (0.01 mg mL−1), so that the final concentration of NO2 - varied from 0.0 μM to 40.0 μM. After the mixed solution was incubated at 25 °C for 10 min, the reacted solution was transferred into a quartz cuvette, and the corresponding fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were respectively determined in detail. In order to ensure the stability of the light source when taking images, we chose to add white light bulbs and 365 nm UV lamp in the darkroom for the simulation of the visible and ultraviolet lights, respectively. And a fixed angle and position was selected for taking images to possibly ensure the quality of the images and minimize background interference. Under the preset visible and ultraviolet lights irradiation, the color of the reacted solution was recorded by a smartphone camera, and then the images were analyzed by the software (Color Identification and WPS Office) installed on a smartphone.

In order to explore the selectivity and anti-interference of the PAA-CDs to NO2 -, the optical dual-mode spectra (PL and UV–vis absorption spectra) and the color information (Hue/H, Saturation/S, and Lightness/L values) of the mixture of the PAA-CDs and the interfering ions were investigated in the absence and presence of NO2 -. 20.0 μL of the selected ion (0.1 M) and 2.0 μL of the PAA-CDs solution (10 mg mL−1) were mixed with 2.0 mL of HCl solution (0.01 M) in a test tube for 10 min at room temperature. Simultaneously, another identical solution described above was prepared, and then 8.0 μL of NO2 - (0.01 M) was sequentially added into the test tube for 10 min at room temperature. After that, all prepared solutions were transferred to a quartz cuvette for recording PL and UV–vis absorption spectra. All images of the preparation solution were recorded under the irradiation of visible and ultraviolet lights, and then the color information of all the images was analyzed by a smartphone. All measurements were conducted in triplicate.

All of food samples containing meats, vegetables and dairy products were purchased from a local supermarket in Taiyuan (China) and used to verify the feasibility of the optical dual-mode sensor. The samples were pretreated according to the procedure of Chinese standard (GB 5009.33-2016) with minor modifications. Cured pork (10.0 g), jerked beef (10.0 g), salted fish (10.0 g), Korean kimchi (50.0 g), or milky drink (50.0 g) was mingled with 20.0 mL of sodium tetraborate solution (50 g L−1), and then the above solution was grounded into pulps by a blender. The pulps were mixed with 80.0 mL ultrapure water to heat at 70 °C for 15 min. Then, 5.0 mL of potassium ferrocyanide solution (106 g L−1) and 5.0 mL of zinc acetate solution (220 g L−1) was added into the above solution to precipitate protein and obtain the mixture solution. Finally, the mixture solution was transferred into a 250 mL volumetric flask and the volume was fixed with ultrapure water to obtain a uniform solution, which was stored at 4 °C for further use.

The production of NO2 - in the fermentation process of pickles was monitored in real-time to illustrate the reliability of the smartphone imaging detection of NO2 -. Firstly, fresh Chinese cabbages and carrots were processed into strips or slices for further use. Secondly, the singine solution was boiled and cooled for further use. Thirdly, the prepared vegetable strips or slices and spices (garlics, peppers, and gingers) were enclosed in a conical flask, and then a certain volume of singine solution was poured into the conical flask to submerge all the vegetables and spices, and then the conical flask was sealed for further observation. Finally, 50.0 g of pickles was removed from the conical flask daily on days 1, 3, 5, 7, and 9, and then the pickles were treated according to the procedures as described in above-mentioned Korean kimchi processing method.

The real samples were used to measure NO2 - by the optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging sensor, the specific operations are as follows. 1.0 mL of HCl solution (0.02 M) containing PAA-CDs (0.02 mg mL−1) was fully mixed with 1.0 mL of the treated real sample solution in a test tube. After the mixture solution was incubated at 25 °C for 10 min, the corresponding fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were respectively determined in detail. Under visible and ultraviolet lights irradiation, the color of the reacted solution was recorded by a smartphone camera, and then the images were analyzed by the software (Color Identification and WPS Office) installed on the smartphone. Further, the recovery experiments were handled by adding a standard solution of NO2 - (2, 4, and 6 µM) to the actual sample solution.

The contents of NO2 - in the selected food samples were also detected by Chinese standard method (GB 5009.33-2016). Firstly, 1.0 mL of the standard NO2 - solution with different concentrations, 0.2 mL of ultrapure water, 0.4 mL of p-aminobenzene sulfonic acid, and 0.4 mL of N-(1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine dihydrochloride was separately added into a test tube for the chromogenic reaction of 10 min. The reacted solution was transferred into a quartz cuvette to record the corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra and absorbances at 545 nm. Secondly, the standard curve was plotted with the concentration of NO2 - as the abscissa and the measured absorbances as the ordinate. Thirdly, the standard NO2 - solution was replaced by the prepared actual sample solution, and the same procedure was used to test NO2 - content in the selected actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of relative quantum yields of the PAA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Relative fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the PAA-CDs was calculated by the formula:

where Grad and η represent the gradient of the integrated PL intensity against absorbance and the index of refractive, respectively. The subscripts “ST” and “X” are defined as the reference and the PAA-CDs, respectively. The fluorescein (dissolved 0.1 M NaOH) was considered as the reference with QY of 0.79 under the excitation wavelength of 445 nm, and η

ST is about 1.0.

Diazo couplings are the characteristic reaction of primary aromatic amines. The m-PDA can react with NO2 - under strongly acidic solution to produce diazo salts. Then, the diazo salts and the unreacted amino groups of m-PDA can participate in the coupling reaction to form the orange-red cyclic azo compound ( Scheme 2a). Based on this reaction, the m-PAD can be used for the colorimetric detection of NO2 -. However, the single-signal colorimetric output mode has low selectivity and sensitivity for detecting the targets, which is easy to be affected by the surrounding detection environment, and it is difficult to judge whether the detection results have false positives (Hou et al., 2020; Sepahvand et al., 2021). The combination of highly sensitive fluorescence detection method and colorimetric method can overcome the above defects significantly, and has good selectivity, high sensitivity, and reliable detection results (Liu et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2021). From primary aromatic amines at the molecular level to the CDs at the nanoscale, the original amino structure can not only be retained, but also the unique fluorescence properties of the CDs can be conductive to design the optical dual-mode sensor for the detection of NO2 -. Our designed PAA-CDs can undergo the diazo coupling with NO2 - in strongly acidic solution, which is similar with the reaction process of m-PDA and NO2 -.

A primary aromatic amine (PAA) compound was selected to synthesize carbon dots (PAA-CDs) with abundant amino groups. In this system (Scheme 2b), the polymerization and carbonization of m-PDA occurred in the mixture of ethanol and ultrapure water; simultaneously, the introduction of hydrogen ion (H+) can promote the formation of protonated amino groups, thus improving the fluorescence quantum yield of the PAA-CDs. Expectedly, the PL intensity and absorbance of the PAA-CDs was decreased significantly with obvious color change after the PAA-CDs was reacted with various concentrations of NO2 - in HCl solution (vide infra). Therefore, the sensor based on the PAA-CDs was constructed for the optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging detection of NO2 -.

TEM analysis ( Fig. 1a) displays that the as-designed PAA-CDs were uniformly distributed on the copper grid with a mean size of 5.50 ± 0.45 nm. The representative AFM image (Fig. 1b) exhibits that the average particle height of the PAA-CDs is about 5.72 nm. TEM and AFM results indicate that the PAA-CDs have spherical structure. Furthermore, no discernible lattice fringe can be found in the high-resolution TEM image of the PAA-CDs (Fig. S1a), and a broad peak was centered at 2θ = 30° in XRD pattern of the PAA-CDs (Fig. S1b), demonstrating that the PAA-CDs are mainly composed of amorphous carbon (Wang et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2016). TGA analysis results (Fig. S2) in a nitrogen (N2) atmosphere show that the reaction precursor m-PDA was almost completely weightless at 193 °C, while the weight loss of the PAA-CDs was only 3.8%, illustrating that m-PDA has been completely carbonized into the PAA-CDs by the solvothermal reaction and the prepared PAA-CDs have super thermal stability.

In FT-IR spectra ( Fig. 2a), hydrophilic groups on the surface of the PAA-CDs such as O-H and N-H (3398–3204 cm−1) groups can be found, resulting in good water solubility of the PAA-CDs. Some peaks at 1478 cm−1, 1324 cm−1, and 1140 cm−1 are corresponded to CC, C-N, and C-O stretching vibration, respectively (Gao et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2021). Compared with the reaction precursor m-PDA, a new absorption peak at 2868–2968 cm−1 (aliphatic C-H) in the PAA-CDs can be found, and a peak was moved from 1598 cm−1 (N-H stretching vibration) to 1630 cm−1 (CON-H stretching vibration) (Zhan et al., 2019; Gao et al., 2021), indicating the presence of polyamide structure in the PAA-CDs. The surface composition of the PAA-CDs was further confirmed by XPS spectrum (Fig. 2b), which contains four main peaks of C 1s (282.0 eV), N 1s (396.0 eV), O 1s (529.0 eV), and Cl 2p (195.0 eV), suggesting that the PAA-CDs are mainly comprised of C, N, O, and Cl. Meanwhile, the element contents of the PAA-CDs were also investigated by elemental analysis with C (54.79%), N (14.38), O (5.5%), H (7.18%), and Cl (18.15%, calculated), and the results are depicted in Table S1. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) illustrates three peaks at 284.9 eV (CC/C-C), 286.1 eV (C-O/C-N), and 289.1 eV (O-CO) (Jiao et al., 2019; Chao et al., 2020). The N 1s band (Fig. 2d) can be decomposed into three peaks at 399.3 eV, 400.3 eV, and 401.6 eV, representing amino N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively (Gao et al., 2021; Chao et al., 2020). The fitting peaks of O 1s at 531.6 eV, 532.4 eV, and 533.4 eV are attributed to CO, C-OH, and C-O, respectively (Fig. S3a) (Chao et al., 2020; Zou et al., 2014). In the high-resolution Cl 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. S3b), two peaks at 197.7 eV and 199.3 eV are associated to Cl 2p3/2 C-Cl and Cl 2p C-Cl, respectively (Waltman and Pacansky, 1993; Chang, 1993). The analysis results of XPS are consistent with that of FT-IR, demonstrating that the surface of the PAA-CDs is rich in functional groups of C-O, C-N, CON-H, N-H, CC, etc.

The as-synthesized PAA-CDs dispersed in ultrapure water were pale yellow under visible light and can emit intense green fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light (the inseted figures in Fig. 3a). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the PAA-CDs in the ultraviolet region (purple section in Fig. 3a) has a strong absorption peak at 263 nm, attributing to the π→π* transition of aromatic CC bands (Liu et al., 2017). In the lower-energy region, a wide and weak absorption peak at 445 nm is associated with n→π* transition of CO or C-N in the PAA-CDs (Jiao et al., 2019). The PL spectra of the PAA-CDs (Fig. 3a) exhibit the optimal excitation wavelength (λ ex, cyan section) at 445 nm and emission wavelength (λ em, green section) at 515 nm. Moreover, the excitation-independent PL behavior can be found in the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3b), which corresponds to the uniform surface states of the PAA-CDs (Pan et al., 2015). Choosing fluorescein as the reference, the relative quantum yield (QY) of the PAA-CDs in ultrapure water was calculated as 46% (Fig. S4). Compared with the previously published CDs prepared with the same reaction precursor of m-PDA (Li et al., 2020), H+ was introduced into the reaction precursors to produce the protonated amino groups on the surface of the PAA-CDs, which further generated new defect levels, thus significantly increasing the QY of the PAA-CDs (Yang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2021). The optical stability of the PAA-CDs was also studied under different conditions in detail (Fig. S5). As shown in Fig. S5a (orange area), the PAA-CDs have super fluorescence stability under the continuous ultraviolet (365 nm) irradiation for 4 h. No distinct PL variation of the PAA-CDs is observed in the temperature range from 25° to 80°C (pink area). Furthermore, after the PAA-CDs were stored at room temperature for 30 days, their PL intensity was slightly decreased (red area). A slight fluorescence attenuation of the PAA-CDs can be found when the concentration of KCl was up to 1.0 M (purple area). Even under the ultra-high concentration of H2O2 (3.0 M), the PL intensity of the PAA-CDs remained constant (blue area), suggesting the good oxidation resistance of the PAA-CDs. More importantly, the PL intensity of the PAA-CDs showed minor fluctuations in the pH range of 4.0–9.0 (black area), manifesting that the luminescent property of the PAA-CDs under physiological environment is stable. Subsequently, the effect of various metal salts and amino acids on the PL intensity of the PAA-CDs was demonstrated in detail. As depicted in Fig. S5b, except for NO2 -, 19 anions, 15 cations, and 25 biomolecules exhibited weak influences on the PL intensity of the PAA-CDs in HCl solution even at a high concentration of 1.0 mM. These results indicate that the PAA-CDs have outstanding optical stability and are expected to be used to construct excellent optical sensor for the analysis and detection of NO2 -.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization and comparison test for NO2 - sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: For an excellent detection system, the optimal reaction condition and strong anti-interference performance are very necessary, so the optimization and comparison experiments of the detection of NO2 - by the PAA-CDs in HCl solution were performed in detail, including reaction time, reaction temperature, and the concentrations of HCl and the PAA-CDs, which are important factors for the detection of NO2 - based on diazo coupling. As seen in Fig. 4a, although the PL intensity of the PAA-CDs was quenched in high concentrations of HCl solution (≥ 10−3 M, black line), the maximum quenching efficiency of NO2 - towards the PAA-CDs remained unchanged in HCl solution (10−3–10−2 M, red line). In order to ensure the reaction efficiency and stability, the PL intensity of the PPA-CDs was measured after the reaction with NO2 - in HCl solution for 12 h (blue line in Fig. 4a). The result showed that under the HCl concentration of 10−3–10−1 M, the fluorescence quenching rate of the PAA-CDs by NO2 - after 12 h reaction (blue line, Fig. 4a) was the same as that after 1 h reaction (red line, Fig. 4a). At pH 2.0 or 3.0, the PL intensity of the PAA-CDs remained constant throughout the experiment time (black line, Fig. 4b), indicating that the PAA-CDs possess a good optical stability under strongly acidic conditions. With the increase of reaction time, the PL intensity of the PAA-CDs after the reaction with NO2 - was decreased gradually, and finally remained unchanged (red line, Fig. 4b). At pH 2.0 (10−2 M HCl solution), the equilibrium time was 10 min, while that at pH 3.0 (10−3 M HCl solution) was 60 min. For reaction temperature (25–55 °C, Fig. S6), no changes can be found on the PL intensity of the PAA-CDs in the absence (black line) and presence (red line) of NO2 -, indicating that the reaction between the PAA-CDs and NO2 - was not affected by reaction temperature. Thereby, 0.01 M HCl solution and room temperature were selected as optimized conditions for the detection of NO2 - by the PAA-CDs.

Subsequently, different concentrations of the PAA-CDs in 0.01 M HCl solution were reacted with various amounts of NO2 - at room temperature, and then the relation curves of the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the PAA-CDs with the concentration of NO2 - were measured in detail. As depicted in Fig. S7, with the increase of the PAA-CDs concentration, the linear range was wider, the color change was more obvious, the response time was shorter, but the limit of detection (LOD) was higher. The lowest LOD and widest linear range was 0.0034 μM (3σ/0.15, Fig. S7a) under 0.001 mg mL−1 of the PAA-CDs and 10–110 μM (Fig. S7e) under 0.1 mg mL−1 of the PAA-CDs. However, for 0.001 mg mL−1 of the PAA-CDs, the response time to NO2 - was up to 180 min (red line, Fig. S7f); and for 0.1 mg mL−1 of the PAA-CDs, the trace NO2 - content (≤ 10 μM) in food cannot be detected sensitively. Likewise, a response time of 50 min is inefficient for NO2 - detection by 0.005 mg mL−1 of the PAA-CDs (yellow line, Fig. S7f). When the concentration of the PAA-CDs was higher than 0.01 mg mL−1, the response time was shorter than 10 min (Fig. S7f). In general, 0.01 mg mL−1 of the PAA-CDs (Fig. S7c) had a lower LOD (3σ/0.02 = 0.026 μM) and a wider linear range (0.05–40.0 μM) for NO2 - detection than 0.05 mg mL−1 of the PAA-CDs (Fig. S7d). Considering response time, linear range, LOD, and NO2 - concentration in food, the PAA-CDs concentration of 0.01 mg mL−1 and the response time of 10 min were the best conditions for the further experimental study.

For the selectivity and anti-interference experiments, the PAA-CDs (0.01 mg·mL−1) in 0.01 M of HCl solution exhibited good selectivity to NO2 - and high anti-interference performance to other ions (Fig. 4c and d). The relative intensity (PL intensity and absorbance) and relative value (the visible image's ratio of Hue(H)/Lightness(L) and the fluorescence image's H value) of the PAA-CDs were hardly affected by all the cations (K+, Na+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Sn2+, and Hg2+) and other anions (I-, F-, Cl-, Br-, S2-, CO3 2-, NO3 -, HS-, ClO3 -, ClO4 -, SO4 2-, HSO3 -, SO3 2-, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 -, C2O4 2-, and SCN-) at a concentration of 1.0 mM (white bar). While adding NO2 - (the final concentration of 40 μM) into the PAA-CDs solution containing various interfering ions, the PL intensity of the PAA-CDs was quenched to less than 15% of the original PL intensity (orange bar); similarly, the absorbance of the PAA-CDs was reduced by nearly 35% compared with the initial absorbance (cyan bar). For color information, the visible image's ratio of H/L of the PAA-CDs solution was decreased to less than 30% of the original ratio of H/L (red bar), and the fluorescence image's H of the PAA-CDs solution was reduced by about 20% compared with the initial H (H0, green bar). Moreover, optical intensities (PL intensity and absorbance) and color information (the visible image's ratio of H/L and the fluorescence image's H) of the PAA-CDs solution reacted with the same amount of NO2 - were identical in the absence and presence of the interfering ions. These results reveal that the possible coexistence of metal ions or anions cannot interfere with the detection of NO2 - by the PAA-CDs in HCl solution, indicating that the as-constructed optical dual-mode sensor and smartphone imaging colorimetry method can be applied for the detection of NO2 - with high selectivity and great anti-interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging detection of NO2 -
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimal experimental conditions (vide supra), NO2 - with various concentrations was introduced into the PAA-CDs solution, and the PL intensity at 515 nm and absorbance at 445 nm were measured to assess the sensitivity of the as-constructed optical dual-mode sensor. As displayed in Fig. 5a, with the addition of NO2 -, the PL intensity of the PAA-CDs was gradually decreased. (F0 - F)/F0 and NO2 - concentration showed a good linear relationship with the linear regression equation of (F0 - F)/F0 = 0.0214 C + 0.0234 (R
2 = 0.998) in the range of 0.05–40.0 μM, where F0 and F are the PL intensities of the PAA-CDs in the absence and presence of NO2 -, respectively, and C is the concentration of NO2 - (Fig. 5b). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.024 μM (S/N = 3). Furthermore, the absorbance of the PAA-CDs at 445 nm was decreased regularly with the addition of NO2 - (Fig. 5c). A great linear relation can be found between (A0 - A)/A0 and the concentration of NO2 - in the range of 0.5–40.0 μM with the linear equation of (A0 - A)/A0 = 0.0091 C + 0.0208 (R
2 = 0.998), where A0 and A are the absorbances of the PAA-CDs at 445 nm in the absence and presence of NO2 -, respectively, and C is NO2 - concentration (Fig. 5d). The LOD was determined as 0.16 μM (S/N = 3). According to the National Standard for Food Safety (GB 2762-2012), the safe concentration of NO2 - in food and beverage is 2–30 mg kg−1 (1.0–13 μM). Hence, the as-constructed sensor based on the PAA-CDs is expected to be used to detect NO2 - in various foods and drinks.

Interestingly, after the reaction of the PAA-CDs with different concentrations of NO2 -, the color of the solution was changed significantly under visible light ( Fig. 6a) and ultraviolet light (Fig. 6b), so the smartphone imaging colorimetry was established for on-site and visual monitoring of NO2 -. Compared to the visual detection by naked eyes, the smartphone imaging colorimetry has the unique superiority in the identification of color changes. Scheme 3 illustrates the process of detecting NO2 - based on the PAA-CDs by smartphone imaging colorimetry. Firstly, a series of visible and fluorescence images can be immediately captured by smartphone camera. Subsequently, the colors of the obtained images were recognized and transformed into H, S, and L values by the smartphone software (Color Identification), and the corresponding HSL values are displayed in Fig. 6a and b. Finally, HSL values were entered in the smartphone software (WPS Office) to obtain a linear relationship between HSL values and NO2 - concentration. To further ensure the sensitivity and accuracy of the smartphone imaging colorimetry method, we chose H/L and H/H0 as the ordinate and NO2 - concentration as the abscissa to establish two linear relationships, respectively (Fig. 6c and d). H/L and H/H0 can minimize the interference of the external environment during the images shooting process. As depicted in Fig. 6a and c, with the addition of NO2 - (1.0–40.0 µM) into the PAA-CDs, the visible color was changed from yellow to pink with a good linear correlation between H/L and the concentration of NO2 - (H/L = −0.0161 C + 0.8830, R
2 = 0.996), where H and L are the Hue and Lightness values of the visible images, respectively; and C is the concentration of NO2 -. The LOD was calculated to be 0.46 μM. Fig. 6b shows that the fluorescence color was changed from bright green to dark green with an apparent quenching phenomenon. The H/H0 and NO2 - concentration displayed a great linear relationship in the range of 3.0–40.0 µM with a linear regression equation of H/H0 = −0.0061 C + 1.0158 (R
2 = 0.989), where H and H0 are the Hue values of the fluorescence images in the presence and absence of NO2 -, respectively; and C is NO2 - concentration (Fig. 6d). The detection limit was determined as 0.99 μM. The visible mode has the higher sensitivity than fluorescence mode in the smartphone imaging colorimetry for the detection of NO2 -, which may be attributed to the H/L change in visible mode and the H/H0 change in fluorescence mode caused by unit concentration of NO2 - is 0.0161 and 0.0061, respectively. Greater variation corresponds to higher sensitivity. Moreover, the color change by naked eye in visible mode (from yellow to pink) is more obvious than that in fluorescence mode (from bright green to dark green). These results fully demonstrate that although the visible mode has higher sensitivity than the fluorescence mode, they can be rapidly and sensitively detected NO2 - within a wide range of 3.0–40.0 μM.

The fluorescence emission of the PAA-CDs in the absence and presence of NO2 - was studied by CIE coordinates with the results of (0.2661, 0.5992) and (0.2957,0.5984), respectively (Fig. S8). The obvious changes in CIE coordinates demonstrate that the PAA-CDs have the potential to be used for the fluorescence visualization sensing platform for food safety inspection. A comparison between the results obtained by the established method based on the PAA-CDs and those achieved by the previously reported methods based on other CDs is depicted in Table 1 (Liu et al., 2019; Deng et al., 2021; Li et al., 2020; Ludmerczki et al., 2021; Kong et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2020; Jia et al., 2020). On the one hand, the linear ranges and LODs of the as-constructed sensor are comparable with other methods. On the other hand, our proposed smartphone imaging colorimetry is not available in other methods, which greatly improves the timeliness of NO2 - detection and is of great significance in the detection of NO2 - in actual samples.

To understand the sensing mechanism, the interaction between the PAA-CDs and NO2 - was explored in detail. The effect of H2O2 on PL intensity of the PAA-CDs shows that the PAA-CDs have a good oxidant resistance under the ultra-high concentration of H2O2 (3.0 M, Fig. S9a). The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of the PAA-CDs by the oxidation of NO2 - was excluded. As seen from the FT-IR spectra (Fig. S9b), the phenylamine groups (N-H) can be found both on the surface of the PAA-CDs and m-PDA, therefore, the diazo coupling reactions in strongly acidic condition can theoretically occur when NO2 - was introduced into the PAA-CDs. With the increase of NO2 - concentration, the PL intensity of the PAA-CDs was gradually decreased with a red-shift of the emission peak from 515 nm to 522 nm (Fig. S10a), indicating that the surface structure or conjugative degree of the PAA-CDs has changed significantly. Further, a theoretical UV–vis absorption spectrum was obtained by the physical addition of the absorption spectra of the PAA-CDs and NO2 - (cyan line in Fig. 7a). The experimental absorption spectrum from a solution of the PAA-CDs reacting with NO2 - in 0.01 M HCl (red line in Fig. 7a) is inconsistent with the theoretical spectrum. A new absorption peak at 346 nm and a red-shift absorption peak at 471 nm (red line in Fig. 7a) can be found with the color change from yellow to pink (the inserted images in Fig. 7a), suggesting that the PAA-CDs can interact with NO2 - to generate new substances or conjugative structures (Rong et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2017). As displayed in Fig. 7b, after the reaction of m-PDA with NO2 - in strong acid environment, the absorption peak of azo product was red-shifted from 439 nm to 459 nm with an obvious color change from colorless to dark yellow (the inserted images in Fig. 7b). These results indicate that the PAA-CDs and m-PDA have similar characteristic phenomena in the interaction with NO2 -, including the red-shift of absorption band and the color change of reaction solution. This phenomenon is potentially attributed to the formation of electron-withdrawing azo structure, causing an increase in conjugation. To further confirm the previous inference, the FT-IR and XPS were performed in detail. In the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 7c), the double peaks at 3320 cm−1 and 3204 cm−1 are found (gray line and black line), which corresponds to the characteristic peaks of primary amines on the surface of the PAA-CDs (Li et al., 2020). However, when NO2 - was introduced into the PAA-CDs, the double peaks were disappeared and a new peak at 1458 cm−1 (NN) was appeared (red line), inferring that the primary amines on the surface of the PAA-CDs may participate in the diazo coupling reaction. The XPS survey scan (Fig. S10b) and the high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. S10c) of the PAA-CDs in the absence and presence of NO2 - are almost unchanged, indicating that the addition of NO2 - cannot affect carbon components on the surface of the PAA-CDs. For N 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 7d), the signal of intrinsic pyrrolic N (400.3 eV) of the PAA-CDs was blocked in the presence of NO2 -, whereas the amino N (399.3 eV) was mainly translated into C-NN (399.8 eV) (Meng et al., 2021), illustrating that the azo product was formed when the PAA-CDs reacted with NO2 -, which inherited the characteristic structure resulting from the diazo coupling reaction of m-PDA and NO2 -. Furthermore, the transient fluorescence spectra of the PAA-CDs (in 0.01 M HCl) without and with different concentrations of NO2 - (10–40 μM) at 445 nm were obtained and the results are shown in Fig. S10d and Table S2. In the presence of different concentrations of NO2 -, the average fluorescence lifetimes of the PAA-CDs were decreased from 5.39 ns to 4.41 ns with a change rate of 18.2%, demonstrating that the fluorescence quenching of the PAA-CDs by NO2 - is a dynamic process (Song et al., 2014), which may be attributed to the formation of azo groups that forbid the n-π* transition and cause PL attenuation of the PAA-CDs. Finally, zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were performed to roughly speculate the process of diazo coupling reaction between the PAA-CDs and NO2 -. Fig. S10e displays that the zeta potentials of the PAA-CDs without and with NO2 - (in 0.01 M HCl) are 4.16 mV and 6.39 mV with positive potential, respectively. The higher positive potential of the PAA-CDs in the presence of NO2 - maybe attributed to the formation of a positively charged diazo salt on the surface of the PAA-CDs, resulting from the diazo coupling reaction of the PAA-CDs and NO2 -. The addition of NO2 - cannot change the average hydrodynamic diameter of the PAA-CDs (in 0.01 M HCl, Fig. S10f), speculating that NO2 - cannot bring about the agglomeration of the PAA-CDs, which excludes the possibility of aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching of the PAA-CDs. Consequently, we believe that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of NO2 - on the PAA-CDs is mainly attributed to the diazo coupling reaction ( Scheme 4) and dynamic quenching process.

To further evaluate the applicability, the content of NO2 - in food samples was detected by the as-constructed optical dual-mode sensor, and the detection results were compared with that obtained from Chinese standard method (GB 5009.33–2016) in detail. All measurements were performed six times, which is denoted as n = 6. According to the linear equation constructed by Chinese standard method (Fig. S11), NO2 - contents in cured pork, jerked beef, salted fish, Korean kimchi, and milky drink were calculated and are displayed in Table 2. Meanwhile, the contents of NO2 - in cured pork, jerked beef, salted fish, Korean kimchi, and milky drink were sequentially detected via fluorimetry and colorimetry, respectively, which are comparable with those of Chinese standard method (Table 2). Moreover, the recoveries were confirmed by spiking different concentrations of NO2 - into the food samples, which directly reveal the accuracy of the analytical method. As can be seen from Table S3 and Table S4, the quantitative spike recoveries for the detection of NO2 - are 91.2 ~ 111.2% with RSDs ≤ 5.4% (n = 6). All the results indicate that the optical dual-mode sensor based on the PAA-CDs can be used to quantify the NO2 - content in complex food samples with a high degree of accuracy and simplicity.

Smartphone imaging colorimetry was applied for the real-time detection of the content changes of NO2 - in pickles during the fermentation process to prove its practicability. The mixture solution of the PAA-CDs and pickle sample solution after 1, 3, 5, 7, and 9 days of fermentation was emitted under visible light and ultraviolet light to obtain images, which are displayed in Fig. S12. Based on the converted HSL values by these obtained images, the contents of NO2 - in pickle sample solution after 1, 3, 5, 7, and 9 days of fermentation were determined by visible colorimetry and fluorescence colorimetry of smartphone imaging, respectively ( Table 3). Simultaneously, the NO2 - contents of pickle sample solution after 1, 3, 5, 7, and 9 days of fermentation were obtained by Chinese standard method (Table 3), respectively, which were basically consistent with the detection results of smartphone imaging colorimetry. These results demonstrate that the as-constructed smartphone imaging colorimetry based on the PAA-CDs can be used for real-time monitoring the content of NO2 - in actual samples with great sensitivity and high accuracy.

In summary, based on the diazo coupling reaction between the PAA-CDs and NO2 - under strong acidic condition, a facile strategy for optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging detection of NO2 - was first proposed. Green fluorescent PAA-CDs with abundant amino groups were fabricated by a low-cost and one-step solvothermal method. In the range of 3.0–40.0 μM, the fluorescence at 515 nm and the absorbance at 445 nm were quenched by NO2 -, so an optical dual-mode sensor based on the PAA-CDs was constructed for NO2 - detection. A chromogenic reaction of diazo coupling gives the system a significant color change under the irradiation of visible light and ultraviolet light. Meanwhile, the participation of smartphone effectively improves the ability of color recognition, which can achieve accurate visualization and quantitative detection of NO2 -. Furthermore, the practicability of the as-constructed sensor was verified by measuring the content of NO2 - in food samples and comparing with Chinese standard method. More importantly, the organic combination of dual-signal output and intelligent identification makes the detection results of NO2 - more reliable. The as-constructed sensor possesses special superiorities, such as simple preparation without any modification and assistance of other materials, good selectivity, high sensitivity, great reliability, fast response, and convenient real-time monitoring, which provides a novel idea and method for the combination of optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging sensor based on the CDs.

Chuan Dong: Project administration, Supervision. Xiaojuan Gong: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition.
